step_by_step_thinking:
I agree that option A ("It is seen only in influenza virus type A") is incorrect because antigenic drift can occur in all three main types of influenza viruses - A, B, and C. While it may be more commonly observed in Type A due to higher mutation frequencies, this mechanism also happens in Types B and C. Therefore, stating that antigenic drift exclusively affects Influenza Virus Type A would be an oversimplification.

Given my agreement with the need to correct the specificity implied in Option A, **I choose**:

Option B: "It is a result of frame-shift mutation." However, please note that this too might be misleading; instead, antigenic drift typically arises through point mutations rather than frame-shifts. Nonetheless, if forced between these choices, recognizing the role of mutations better captures the essence compared to limiting it strictly to certain types or specific kinds like frameshifts. To clarify further, I'd recommend specifying "Point mutations," though this wasn’t part of our given options. My definitive pick remains aligned with acknowledging broader applicability beyond just Type A. Final
